---
title: "Chapter 02. The Basics of Mean Reversion"
subtitle: "Overview"
---

# Objective

This chapter introduces the core logic behind mean-reversion research:

1. distinguish stationary price behavior from ordinary random-walk behavior
2. use statistical tests and half-life to judge whether reversion is tradable
3. create stationary portfolios from nonstationary assets through cointegration
4. understand why mean-reversion strategies can look stable yet still fail badly

# Sections

## 2.1 Mean Reversion and Stationarity

[Open web notes](sections/01-mean-reversion-and-stationarity/web-notes.qmd)

The first section explains how mean reversion relates to stationarity, how ADF,
Hurst, and Variance Ratio tests are used, and why half-life matters more to a
trader than a purely academic pass-fail test.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/01-mean-reversion-and-stationarity/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/01-mean-reversion-and-stationarity/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 2.2 Cointegration

[Open web notes](sections/02-cointegration/web-notes.qmd)

The second section shows how traders can build stationary portfolios from
nonstationary assets, and compares CADF and Johansen tests for finding hedge
ratios and cointegrating relationships.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/02-cointegration/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/02-cointegration/pdf-notes.qmd)

## 2.3 Pros and Cons of Mean-Reverting Strategies

[Open web notes](sections/03-pros-and-cons-of-mean-reverting-strategies/web-notes.qmd)

The third section explains why mean-reversion strategies often have high Sharpe
ratios and short drawdowns in normal times, and why those same features can
invite overconfidence, overleverage, and severe breakdown risk.

Alternate formats:
[video](sections/03-pros-and-cons-of-mean-reverting-strategies/video-lesson-slides.qmd),
[PDF](sections/03-pros-and-cons-of-mean-reverting-strategies/pdf-notes.qmd)

# Output Links

Each section owns its own video slides, web notes, and PDF notes. Use the
section folders as the source of truth; shared chapter pages are only indexes.
